missing screenshot: artifacts/r9-no-folder-file.openWelcomeClose.before.png

450 / 564 · r9-no-folder-file.openWelcomeClose.before.png

Close the active (Welcome) tab → open tab count shrinks

no-folder · No folder — empty workspace (workspace-needing behaviours SKIP)
file.openWelcomeClose
PASS
happened
openTabs 1 → 0
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/files.mjs
timing
{"effect":30400}
machine
{"procs":"10→10","memMiB":4,"fsChanges":66,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":15000,"tx":10000}}
look for
WHAT: On a fresh workbench (where the VS Code Welcome/Get-Started tab is open by
default), captures `openTabs.length`, fires the real `workbench.action.closeActiveEditor`
command, then asserts the count strictly decreased (afterN < beforeN). If the snapshot
doesn't expose `openTabs` at all it reports "not measurable" rather than asserting.

WHY THIS IS CORRECT: `closeActiveEditor` closes exactly the focused editor/tab; on a
fresh window the focused tab IS the Welcome tab, so the open-tab set must shrink by one.
We assert a *delta* (after < before) rather than a hard "tabs == N" because the exact
fresh-window tab count is environment-dependent (extensions, restored editors) — the
only invariant we can rely on is "closing removed one". The measurable-guard exists
because this behaviour runs with baseline {command,query} caps only and must degrade
honestly if the snapshot shape changes.

WHY IT MATTERS: This is the smoke test (tagged "smoke") that proves the command channel
round-trips a genuine workbench mutation AND that the snapshot reflects editor lifecycle
changes. If a refactor breaks the bridge's command dispatch, or makes `openTabs` stale
(cached and never invalidated on close), this is the cheapest, earliest tripwire — a
break here implies the entire observe→act→observe loop is untrustworthy for every other
files test, so it's deliberately kept dependency-free.
evidence
{
  "beforeTabs": [
    "Welcome"
  ],
  "afterTabs": []
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r6-python-view.toggleSidebar.before.png

453 / 564 · r6-python-view.toggleSidebar.before.png

View: Toggle Primary Side Bar

python · +python — python toolchain + Python extension
view.toggleSidebar
PASS
happened
toggleSidebarVisibility resolved (visibility not yet in Snapshot)
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/viewsSettings.mjs
timing
{"effect":14307}
machine
{"procs":"11→11","memMiB":-83,"fsChanges":66,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":8000,"tx":0}}
look for
WHAT: Drives the real VS Code command 'workbench.action.toggleSidebarVisibility'
through the bridge (env.act, which throws on a non-ok executeCommand result) and
asserts only that the command resolved. We snapshot visibleEditors before and
after as evidence; we do NOT assert sidebar visibility itself because the Snapshot
contract (§3.3) does not yet expose it (a Track-D upgrade may add it).

WHY THIS OUTCOME: The primary side bar is a workbench-chrome element, not an
editor or a terminal. Toggling its visibility is a pure layout operation — it does
not open/close editors, so visibleEditors is expected to be stable across the
toggle. The only thing we can faithfully verify today is that VS Code accepted and
executed the built-in command id without error, which env.act guarantees by
rejecting on !ok. Asserting pass:true unconditionally is correct here precisely
because the success signal IS "the command resolved"; inventing a visibility
assertion against a field the bridge can't supply would be a false test.

WHY IT MATTERS: This guards the command-dispatch path itself — that the bridge can
still reach the VS Code command registry and run a stock workbench command end to
end. If a refactor of the bridge transport, the executeCommand wrapper, or the
command-id wiring breaks, env.act will throw and this smoke test goes red even
though it makes no rich assertion. A future reader seeing this fail should suspect
the bridge/command plumbing, not the sidebar feature. The recorded before/after
visibleEditors also documents the baseline assumption (sidebar toggling is
editor-neutral) so that if Track-D later adds a real visibility check, the expected
invariants are already written down.
evidence
{
  "beforeVisibleEditors": [],
  "afterVisibleEditors": []
}

{"effect":26262}
machine
{"procs":"10→10","memMiB":0,"fsChanges":72,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":14000,"tx":10000}}
look for
WHAT: On a fresh workbench (where the VS Code Welcome/Get-Started tab is open by
default), captures `openTabs.length`, fires the real `workbench.action.closeActiveEditor`
command, then asserts the count strictly decreased (afterN < beforeN). If the snapshot
doesn't expose `openTabs` at all it reports "not measurable" rather than asserting.

WHY THIS IS CORRECT: `closeActiveEditor` closes exactly the focused editor/tab; on a
fresh window the focused tab IS the Welcome tab, so the open-tab set must shrink by one.
We assert a *delta* (after < before) rather than a hard "tabs == N" because the exact
fresh-window tab count is environment-dependent (extensions, restored editors) — the
only invariant we can rely on is "closing removed one". The measurable-guard exists
because this behaviour runs with baseline {command,query} caps only and must degrade
honestly if the snapshot shape changes.

WHY IT MATTERS: This is the smoke test (tagged "smoke") that proves the command channel
round-trips a genuine workbench mutation AND that the snapshot reflects editor lifecycle
changes. If a refactor breaks the bridge's command dispatch, or makes `openTabs` stale
(cached and never invalidated on close), this is the cheapest, earliest tripwire — a
break here implies the entire observe→act→observe loop is untrustworthy for every other
files test, so it's deliberately kept dependency-free.
evidence
{
  "beforeTabs": [
    "Welcome"
  ],
  "afterTabs": []
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r6-python-view.togglePanel.before.png

459 / 564 · r6-python-view.togglePanel.before.png

View: Toggle Panel

python · +python — python toolchain + Python extension
view.togglePanel
PASS
happened
togglePanel resolved (terminalCount 1 → 1, unchanged by hide)
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/viewsSettings.mjs
timing
{"effect":18330}
machine
{"procs":"11→11","memMiB":1,"fsChanges":66,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":7000,"tx":0}}
look for
WHAT: Runs 'workbench.action.togglePanel' via env.act and asserts the command
resolved, capturing terminalCount before and after as evidence. We explicitly do
NOT assert that the panel's visibility changed (no Snapshot field for it yet) and
we explicitly assert that terminalCount is the relevant invariant to surface.

WHY THIS OUTCOME: The bottom panel is the host surface for the integrated terminal
(among other views). A common but wrong mental model is "hiding the panel kills the
terminal" — in VS Code the terminal process keeps running when its hosting panel is
toggled closed; only the UI is hidden. So terminalCount is expected to be UNCHANGED
across the toggle. That is why the detail line reports the count transition as
"unchanged by hide": the correct behaviour is that toggling the panel is a
visibility-only operation with no lifecycle effect on terminals. Asserting pass on
command resolution is the honest assertion given the Snapshot can't report panel
visibility today.

WHY IT MATTERS: This pins down the contract that panel visibility and terminal
lifecycle are decoupled. If a refactor (e.g. of terminal disposal, of the
panel-show/hide handlers, or of how the env counts terminals) ever caused hiding
the panel to dispose terminals, terminalCount would drift and the evidence here
would expose it even before a dedicated visibility check exists. A future reader
debugging a terminal-count regression can use the before/after evidence to decide
whether the panel toggle is implicated. It also guards the same command-dispatch
plumbing as toggleSidebar — a thrown env.act means the bridge lost the ability to
run stock workbench commands.
evidence
{
  "beforeTerminalCount": 1,
  "afterTerminalCount": 1
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r7-node-terminal.new.before.png

462 / 564 · r7-node-terminal.new.before.png

Terminal: New Terminal opens a terminal

node · +node — Node toolchain + JS/TS extension
terminal.new
PASS
happened
terminals 0 → 1 (["bash"])
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/terminal.mjs
timing
{"effect":28293}
machine
{"procs":"10→12","memMiB":-49,"fsChanges":71,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":133000,"tx":200000}}
look for
WHAT: Snapshots terminalCount, fires the built-in command
'workbench.action.terminal.new' through the bridge act() channel, waits 2s, and
asserts the post-action terminalCount is strictly greater than the pre-action
count. This is the single most basic terminal assertion in the suite — "an
integrated terminal actually came into existence when we asked for one."

WHY THIS IS THE EXPECTED OUTCOME: 'workbench.action.terminal.new' is VS Code's
canonical command for creating an integrated terminal. When invoked it spawns a
backing pty/shell process and registers a Terminal object with the workbench;
our query bridge reads window.terminals and reports terminalCount. So a healthy
env must show count rising by (at least) one. We assert ">" rather than "== +1"
deliberately: this is the SHARED-isolation smoke test, so the env may already
carry terminals from earlier behaviours — the only invariant we can rely on is
monotonic growth, not an exact value. The 2s sleep covers the async gap between
the command resolving and the pty registering in the snapshot.

WHY IT MATTERS: This is the proven baseline ported straight from harness.mjs
(terminalCount 0→1) and the canary for the entire bridge round-trip — if act()
can dispatch a command, VS Code can execute it, and query() can observe the
result, then the whole command→effect→observation pipeline is alive. If this
ever breaks after a refactor, suspect the bridge wiring (act/query channel,
command id rename, or the query no longer reading window.terminals) BEFORE
suspecting terminals themselves; every richer terminal behaviour builds on this
exact mechanism, so a green here narrows a failure elsewhere to that behaviour's
own specifics.
evidence
{
  "before": {
    "terminalCount": 0,
    "terminals": []
  },
  "after": {
    "terminalCount": 1,
    "terminals": [
      "bash"
    ]
  }
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r9-no-folder-search.findInFiles.before.png

465 / 564 · r9-no-folder-search.findInFiles.before.png

Search: open the Find-in-Files view

no-folder · No folder — empty workspace (workspace-needing behaviours SKIP)
search.findInFiles
PASS
happened
executeCommand(findInFiles) returned ok (no view in snapshot to assert)
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/searchGit.mjs
timing
{"effect":22368}
machine
{"procs":"10→10","memMiB":0,"fsChanges":66,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":12000,"tx":10000}}
look for
WHAT: Observes the active/focused view before, executes the built-in
"workbench.action.findInFiles" command via the bridge, waits 1.2s for the view
to settle, then re-observes. If the snapshot exposes activeView/focusedView, it
asserts that view matches /search/i; if the snapshot can't tell us the view, it
degrades to "the command ran ok" (env.act throws on a non-ok reply, so reaching
the return is the assertion). Evidence records before/after view names.

WHY THIS IS THE EXPECTED OUTCOME: "workbench.action.findInFiles" is the canonical
command that reveals the Search viewlet in the sidebar and focuses its query box.
On a healthy workbench it always succeeds and, when the view layer is queryable,
the active view becomes the Search view — hence the /search/i match. The dual
posture (assert-if-known, else ok) is intentional: Track-E may or may not surface
the active view in the snapshot, and we refuse to hard-fail a real success just
because the harness can't see the viewlet name. That mirrors the proven
palette.open baseline.

WHY IT MATTERS: This guards the Search feature's entry point. If a refactor
unregisters/renames the command, breaks viewlet activation, or the snapshot's
view-reporting drifts, this test catches it. A future reader seeing the /search/i
branch fail should check whether the workbench still maps findInFiles to the
Search viewlet (or whether the snapshot's view field changed shape); seeing the
fallback branch always taken means the snapshot lost its view introspection and
the assertion has silently weakened — worth restoring.
evidence
{
  "beforeView": null,
  "afterView": null
}

[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/viewsSettings.mjs
timing
{"effect":16151}
machine
{"procs":"11→11","memMiB":1,"fsChanges":66,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":9000,"tx":0}}
look for
WHAT: Invokes 'workbench.actions.view.problems' via env.act to open the Problems
(Errors and Warnings) view, then observes and surfaces the current diagnostics
count. The assertion is that the command resolved; the diagnostics count is
reported as evidence (with an "n/a" fallback when the Snapshot omits it).

WHY THIS OUTCOME: Opening the Problems view is a focus/reveal operation — it shows
the diagnostics panel but does not itself create or clear diagnostics. The Snapshot
contract (§3.3) does not expose which view currently has focus, so there is no
faithful way to assert "the Problems view is now focused"; the only guaranteed
truth is that VS Code accepted the built-in command id and ran it, which env.act
enforces by throwing on !ok. Reporting diagnostics (rather than asserting on it) is
correct because the count depends entirely on the scenario's workspace state and is
not something this command changes — so it is evidence, not an assertion target.

WHY IT MATTERS: This is a smoke test for the Problems-view command id and the
diagnostics field of the Snapshot. If VS Code renames/retires the command, or a
bridge refactor breaks command dispatch, env.act throws and this goes red — telling
a future reader the failure is in command wiring, not in diagnostics collection.
The surfaced diagnostics count also doubles as a cheap probe that the bridge's
diagnostics plumbing is alive; an unexpected "n/a" where a number was expected hints
the Snapshot's diagnostics field regressed, independent of the Problems command
itself.
evidence
{
  "diagnostics": 0
}

WHY IT MATTERS: This is a smoke test for the Problems-view command id and the

missing screenshot: artifacts/r7-node-view.toggleSidebar.before.png

473 / 564 · r7-node-view.toggleSidebar.before.png

View: Toggle Primary Side Bar

node · +node — Node toolchain + JS/TS extension
view.toggleSidebar
PASS
happened
toggleSidebarVisibility resolved (visibility not yet in Snapshot)
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/viewsSettings.mjs
timing
{"effect":24026}
machine
{"procs":"12→12","memMiB":1,"fsChanges":71,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":13000,"tx":10000}}
look for
WHAT: Drives the real VS Code command 'workbench.action.toggleSidebarVisibility'
through the bridge (env.act, which throws on a non-ok executeCommand result) and
asserts only that the command resolved. We snapshot visibleEditors before and
after as evidence; we do NOT assert sidebar visibility itself because the Snapshot
contract (§3.3) does not yet expose it (a Track-D upgrade may add it).

WHY THIS OUTCOME: The primary side bar is a workbench-chrome element, not an
editor or a terminal. Toggling its visibility is a pure layout operation — it does
not open/close editors, so visibleEditors is expected to be stable across the
toggle. The only thing we can faithfully verify today is that VS Code accepted and
executed the built-in command id without error, which env.act guarantees by
rejecting on !ok. Asserting pass:true unconditionally is correct here precisely
because the success signal IS "the command resolved"; inventing a visibility
assertion against a field the bridge can't supply would be a false test.

WHY IT MATTERS: This guards the command-dispatch path itself — that the bridge can
still reach the VS Code command registry and run a stock workbench command end to
end. If a refactor of the bridge transport, the executeCommand wrapper, or the
command-id wiring breaks, env.act will throw and this smoke test goes red even
though it makes no rich assertion. A future reader seeing this fail should suspect
the bridge/command plumbing, not the sidebar feature. The recorded before/after
visibleEditors also documents the baseline assumption (sidebar toggling is
editor-neutral) so that if Track-D later adds a real visibility check, the expected
invariants are already written down.
evidence
{
  "beforeVisibleEditors": [],
  "afterVisibleEditors": []
}

happened

missing screenshot: artifacts/r8-rust-terminal.new.before.png

476 / 564 · r8-rust-terminal.new.before.png

Terminal: New Terminal opens a terminal

rust · +rust — Rust toolchain + rust-analyzer extension
terminal.new
PASS
happened
terminals 0 → 1 (["bash"])
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/terminal.mjs
timing
{"effect":24164}
machine
{"procs":"10→12","memMiB":-52,"fsChanges":74,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":138000,"tx":200000}}
look for
WHAT: Snapshots terminalCount, fires the built-in command
'workbench.action.terminal.new' through the bridge act() channel, waits 2s, and
asserts the post-action terminalCount is strictly greater than the pre-action
count. This is the single most basic terminal assertion in the suite — "an
integrated terminal actually came into existence when we asked for one."

WHY THIS IS THE EXPECTED OUTCOME: 'workbench.action.terminal.new' is VS Code's
canonical command for creating an integrated terminal. When invoked it spawns a
backing pty/shell process and registers a Terminal object with the workbench;
our query bridge reads window.terminals and reports terminalCount. So a healthy
env must show count rising by (at least) one. We assert ">" rather than "== +1"
deliberately: this is the SHARED-isolation smoke test, so the env may already
carry terminals from earlier behaviours — the only invariant we can rely on is
monotonic growth, not an exact value. The 2s sleep covers the async gap between
the command resolving and the pty registering in the snapshot.

WHY IT MATTERS: This is the proven baseline ported straight from harness.mjs
(terminalCount 0→1) and the canary for the entire bridge round-trip — if act()
can dispatch a command, VS Code can execute it, and query() can observe the
result, then the whole command→effect→observation pipeline is alive. If this
ever breaks after a refactor, suspect the bridge wiring (act/query channel,
command id rename, or the query no longer reading window.terminals) BEFORE
suspecting terminals themselves; every richer terminal behaviour builds on this
exact mechanism, so a green here narrows a failure elsewhere to that behaviour's
own specifics.
evidence
{
  "before": {
    "terminalCount": 0,
    "terminals": []
  },
  "after": {
    "terminalCount": 1,
    "terminals": [
      "bash"
    ]
  }
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r8-rust-terminal.new.after.png

missing screenshot: artifacts/r6-python-settings.toggleMinimap.png

482 / 564 · r6-python-settings.toggleMinimap.png

Settings: Toggle Minimap flips editor.minimap.enabled

python · +python — python toolchain + Python extension
settings.toggleMinimap
PASS
happened
editor.minimap.enabled true → false
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/viewsSettings.mjs
timing
{"effect":23997}
machine
{"procs":"11→12","memMiB":47,"fsChanges":76,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":71000,"tx":270000}}
look for
WHAT: Reads editor.minimap.enabled via the 'setting' bridge query, runs
'editor.action.toggleMinimap' via env.act, re-reads the setting, and asserts the
value actually flipped (before !== after && after !== undefined). Unlike the other
behaviours in this file, this one makes a REAL round-trip assertion on observable
state, so it declares needs:["setting"] and is SKIPPED (not failed) when the bridge
does not advertise the 'setting' capability.

WHY THIS OUTCOME: Minimap was chosen deliberately over word-wrap. toggleWordWrap
sets a transient PER-EDITOR override that config.get / the 'setting' query never
reflect, so it is fundamentally unverifiable through this path — a test on it would
either always pass vacuously or always fail. toggleMinimap, by contrast, mutates
the actual editor.minimap.enabled configuration value, which the 'setting' query
reads back. Because it is a boolean toggle, a correct implementation MUST produce
before !== after; if the value is undefined afterward, the setting wasn't readable
at all. The settingValue() helper tolerates both result shapes (top-level r.value
and nested r.data.value per §3.3) so the assertion doesn't hinge on which Track-E
encoding the bridge happens to use.

WHY IT MATTERS: This is the one end-to-end proof in the file that a settings-mutating
command's effect is observable through the bridge's read path — command write +
setting read, the full loop. It guards three things at once: (1) the toggleMinimap
command still maps to editor.minimap.enabled, (2) the 'setting' query returns the
live value in a shape settingValue() understands, and (3) the choice of a
config-backed (not per-editor-override) setting stays correct. A future reader
seeing this fail should check, in order: did the setting-query result shape change
(after===undefined), or did the command stop mutating the config (before===after)?
The needs:["setting"] gate ensures that on a bridge without the capability this
SKIPS cleanly rather than producing a misleading red.
evidence
{
  "before": true,
  "after": false
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r6-python-settings.toggleMinimap.result.png

look for

reflect, so it is fundamentally unverifiable through this path — a test on it would
either always pass vacuously or always fail. toggleMinimap, by contrast, mutates
the actual editor.minimap.enabled configuration value, which the 'setting' query
reads back. Because it is a boolean toggle, a correct implementation MUST produce
before !== after; if the value is undefined afterward, the setting wasn't readable
at all. The settingValue() helper tolerates both result shapes (top-level r.value
and nested r.data.value per §3.3) so the assertion doesn't hinge on which Track-E
encoding the bridge happens to use.

WHY IT MATTERS: This is the one end-to-end proof in the file that a settings-mutating
command's effect is observable through the bridge's read path — command write +
setting read, the full loop. It guards three things at once: (1) the toggleMinimap
command still maps to editor.minimap.enabled, (2) the 'setting' query returns the
live value in a shape settingValue() understands, and (3) the choice of a
config-backed (not per-editor-override) setting stays correct. A future reader
seeing this fail should check, in order: did the setting-query result shape change
(after===undefined), or did the command stop mutating the config (before===after)?
The needs:["setting"] gate ensures that on a bridge without the capability this
SKIPS cleanly rather than producing a misleading red.
evidence
{
  "before": true,
  "after": false
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r7-node-view.togglePanel.before.png

484 / 564 · r7-node-view.togglePanel.before.png

View: Toggle Panel

node · +node — Node toolchain + JS/TS extension
view.togglePanel
PASS
happened
togglePanel resolved (terminalCount 1 → 1, unchanged by hide)
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/viewsSettings.mjs
timing
{"effect":22359}
machine
{"procs":"12→12","memMiB":0,"fsChanges":71,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":11000,"tx":10000}}
look for
WHAT: Runs 'workbench.action.togglePanel' via env.act and asserts the command
resolved, capturing terminalCount before and after as evidence. We explicitly do
NOT assert that the panel's visibility changed (no Snapshot field for it yet) and
we explicitly assert that terminalCount is the relevant invariant to surface.

WHY THIS OUTCOME: The bottom panel is the host surface for the integrated terminal
(among other views). A common but wrong mental model is "hiding the panel kills the
terminal" — in VS Code the terminal process keeps running when its hosting panel is
toggled closed; only the UI is hidden. So terminalCount is expected to be UNCHANGED
across the toggle. That is why the detail line reports the count transition as
"unchanged by hide": the correct behaviour is that toggling the panel is a
visibility-only operation with no lifecycle effect on terminals. Asserting pass on
command resolution is the honest assertion given the Snapshot can't report panel
visibility today.

WHY IT MATTERS: This pins down the contract that panel visibility and terminal
lifecycle are decoupled. If a refactor (e.g. of terminal disposal, of the
panel-show/hide handlers, or of how the env counts terminals) ever caused hiding
the panel to dispose terminals, terminalCount would drift and the evidence here
would expose it even before a dedicated visibility check exists. A future reader
debugging a terminal-count regression can use the before/after evidence to decide
whether the panel toggle is implicated. It also guards the same command-dispatch
plumbing as toggleSidebar — a thrown env.act means the bridge lost the ability to
run stock workbench commands.
evidence
{
  "beforeTerminalCount": 1,
  "afterTerminalCount": 1
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r8-rust-view.toggleSidebar.before.png

487 / 564 · r8-rust-view.toggleSidebar.before.png

View: Toggle Primary Side Bar

rust · +rust — Rust toolchain + rust-analyzer extension
view.toggleSidebar
PASS
happened
toggleSidebarVisibility resolved (visibility not yet in Snapshot)
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/viewsSettings.mjs
timing
{"effect":20358}
machine
{"procs":"12→12","memMiB":1,"fsChanges":74,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":11000,"tx":10000}}
look for
WHAT: Drives the real VS Code command 'workbench.action.toggleSidebarVisibility'
through the bridge (env.act, which throws on a non-ok executeCommand result) and
asserts only that the command resolved. We snapshot visibleEditors before and
after as evidence; we do NOT assert sidebar visibility itself because the Snapshot
contract (§3.3) does not yet expose it (a Track-D upgrade may add it).

WHY THIS OUTCOME: The primary side bar is a workbench-chrome element, not an
editor or a terminal. Toggling its visibility is a pure layout operation — it does
not open/close editors, so visibleEditors is expected to be stable across the
toggle. The only thing we can faithfully verify today is that VS Code accepted and
executed the built-in command id without error, which env.act guarantees by
rejecting on !ok. Asserting pass:true unconditionally is correct here precisely
because the success signal IS "the command resolved"; inventing a visibility
assertion against a field the bridge can't supply would be a false test.

WHY IT MATTERS: This guards the command-dispatch path itself — that the bridge can
still reach the VS Code command registry and run a stock workbench command end to
end. If a refactor of the bridge transport, the executeCommand wrapper, or the
command-id wiring breaks, env.act will throw and this smoke test goes red even
though it makes no rich assertion. A future reader seeing this fail should suspect
the bridge/command plumbing, not the sidebar feature. The recorded before/after
visibleEditors also documents the baseline assumption (sidebar toggling is
editor-neutral) so that if Track-D later adds a real visibility check, the expected
invariants are already written down.
evidence
{
  "beforeVisibleEditors": [],
  "afterVisibleEditors": []
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r7-node-problems.open.png

493 / 564 · r7-node-problems.open.png

View: Open Problems (Errors and Warnings)

node · +node — Node toolchain + JS/TS extension
problems.open
PASS
happened
Problems view opened (diagnostics: 0)
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/viewsSettings.mjs
timing
{"effect":12001}
machine
{"procs":"12→12","memMiB":0,"fsChanges":71,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":6000,"tx":0}}
look for
WHAT: Invokes 'workbench.actions.view.problems' via env.act to open the Problems
(Errors and Warnings) view, then observes and surfaces the current diagnostics
count. The assertion is that the command resolved; the diagnostics count is
reported as evidence (with an "n/a" fallback when the Snapshot omits it).

WHY THIS OUTCOME: Opening the Problems view is a focus/reveal operation — it shows
the diagnostics panel but does not itself create or clear diagnostics. The Snapshot
contract (§3.3) does not expose which view currently has focus, so there is no
faithful way to assert "the Problems view is now focused"; the only guaranteed
truth is that VS Code accepted the built-in command id and ran it, which env.act
enforces by throwing on !ok. Reporting diagnostics (rather than asserting on it) is
correct because the count depends entirely on the scenario's workspace state and is
not something this command changes — so it is evidence, not an assertion target.

WHY IT MATTERS: This is a smoke test for the Problems-view command id and the
diagnostics field of the Snapshot. If VS Code renames/retires the command, or a
bridge refactor breaks command dispatch, env.act throws and this goes red — telling
a future reader the failure is in command wiring, not in diagnostics collection.
The surfaced diagnostics count also doubles as a cheap probe that the bridge's
diagnostics plumbing is alive; an unexpected "n/a" where a number was expected hints
the Snapshot's diagnostics field regressed, independent of the Problems command
itself.
evidence
{
  "diagnostics": 0
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r6-python-visual.workbenchTabSmoke.full.png

495 / 564 · r6-python-visual.workbenchTabSmoke.full.png

Visual: workbench renders with an editor tab and nonblank screenshot

python · +python — python toolchain + Python extension
visual.workbenchTabSmoke
PASS
happened
workbench visible with 1 tab(s); screenshot 1280x720, blackish=0.0003, buckets=67
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/visualSmoke.mjs
timing
{"effect":28371}
machine
{"procs":"12→12","memMiB":0,"fsChanges":77,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":17000,"tx":10000}}
look for
WHAT: Boots one Fleet environment, writes and opens a real file so VS Code must
show an editor tab, then uses Playwright DOM checks plus a decoded screenshot to
assert that the workbench chrome is present and the captured page is not blank or
all-black.

WHY THIS IS THE EXPECTED OUTCOME: A healthy VS Code / code-server workbench with
one opened file has a .monaco-workbench root, an editor part, a tab row, an
activity bar, and a status bar. Its screenshot contains many colour buckets and
not an overwhelming fraction of near-black pixels. Those are stable visual facts
that do not depend on exact theme colours or text rendering.

WHY IT MATTERS: Bridge command/query tests can pass while the user-facing page is
visually broken. This catches the class of regressions where the editor starts
and phones home but the browser surface is blank, black, missing tabs, or missing
core workbench chrome. It is intentionally a coarse smoke test: exact pixel
matching would be brittle across VS Code/theme/browser versions.
evidence
{
  "screenshot": "artifacts/r6-python-visual.workbenchTabSmoke.full.png",
  "tabCount": 1,
  "activityBarCount": 1,
  "editorCount": 1,
  "statusBarCount": 1,
  "stats": {
    "decoded": true,
    "width": 1280,
    "height": 720,
    "blackishRatio": 0.0003,
    "lightRatio": 0.9884,
    "colorBuckets": 67
  }
}

evidence
{
  "screenshot": "artifacts/r6-python-visual.workbenchTabSmoke.full.png",
  "tabCount": 1,
  "activityBarCount": 1,
  "editorCount": 1,
  "statusBarCount": 1,
  "stats": {
    "decoded": true,
    "width": 1280,
    "height": 720,
    "blackishRatio": 0.0003,
    "lightRatio": 0.9884,
    "colorBuckets": 67
  }
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r8-rust-view.togglePanel.before.png

497 / 564 · r8-rust-view.togglePanel.before.png

View: Toggle Panel

rust · +rust — Rust toolchain + rust-analyzer extension
view.togglePanel
PASS
happened
togglePanel resolved (terminalCount 1 → 1, unchanged by hide)
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/viewsSettings.mjs
timing
{"effect":20344}
machine
{"procs":"12→12","memMiB":1,"fsChanges":74,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":10000,"tx":10000}}
look for
WHAT: Runs 'workbench.action.togglePanel' via env.act and asserts the command
resolved, capturing terminalCount before and after as evidence. We explicitly do
NOT assert that the panel's visibility changed (no Snapshot field for it yet) and
we explicitly assert that terminalCount is the relevant invariant to surface.

WHY THIS OUTCOME: The bottom panel is the host surface for the integrated terminal
(among other views). A common but wrong mental model is "hiding the panel kills the
terminal" — in VS Code the terminal process keeps running when its hosting panel is
toggled closed; only the UI is hidden. So terminalCount is expected to be UNCHANGED
across the toggle. That is why the detail line reports the count transition as
"unchanged by hide": the correct behaviour is that toggling the panel is a
visibility-only operation with no lifecycle effect on terminals. Asserting pass on
command resolution is the honest assertion given the Snapshot can't report panel
visibility today.

WHY IT MATTERS: This pins down the contract that panel visibility and terminal
lifecycle are decoupled. If a refactor (e.g. of terminal disposal, of the
panel-show/hide handlers, or of how the env counts terminals) ever caused hiding
the panel to dispose terminals, terminalCount would drift and the evidence here
would expose it even before a dedicated visibility check exists. A future reader
debugging a terminal-count regression can use the before/after evidence to decide
whether the panel toggle is implicated. It also guards the same command-dispatch
plumbing as toggleSidebar — a thrown env.act means the bridge lost the ability to
run stock workbench commands.
evidence
{
  "beforeTerminalCount": 1,
  "afterTerminalCount": 1
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r7-node-settings.toggleMinimap.png

503 / 564 · r7-node-settings.toggleMinimap.png

Settings: Toggle Minimap flips editor.minimap.enabled

node · +node — Node toolchain + JS/TS extension
settings.toggleMinimap
PASS
happened
editor.minimap.enabled true → false
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/viewsSettings.mjs
timing
{"effect":15955}
machine
{"procs":"12→13","memMiB":27,"fsChanges":78,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":62000,"tx":120000}}
look for
WHAT: Reads editor.minimap.enabled via the 'setting' bridge query, runs
'editor.action.toggleMinimap' via env.act, re-reads the setting, and asserts the
value actually flipped (before !== after && after !== undefined). Unlike the other
behaviours in this file, this one makes a REAL round-trip assertion on observable
state, so it declares needs:["setting"] and is SKIPPED (not failed) when the bridge
does not advertise the 'setting' capability.

WHY THIS OUTCOME: Minimap was chosen deliberately over word-wrap. toggleWordWrap
sets a transient PER-EDITOR override that config.get / the 'setting' query never
reflect, so it is fundamentally unverifiable through this path — a test on it would
either always pass vacuously or always fail. toggleMinimap, by contrast, mutates
the actual editor.minimap.enabled configuration value, which the 'setting' query
reads back. Because it is a boolean toggle, a correct implementation MUST produce
before !== after; if the value is undefined afterward, the setting wasn't readable
at all. The settingValue() helper tolerates both result shapes (top-level r.value
and nested r.data.value per §3.3) so the assertion doesn't hinge on which Track-E
encoding the bridge happens to use.

WHY IT MATTERS: This is the one end-to-end proof in the file that a settings-mutating
command's effect is observable through the bridge's read path — command write +
setting read, the full loop. It guards three things at once: (1) the toggleMinimap
command still maps to editor.minimap.enabled, (2) the 'setting' query returns the
live value in a shape settingValue() understands, and (3) the choice of a
config-backed (not per-editor-override) setting stays correct. A future reader
seeing this fail should check, in order: did the setting-query result shape change
(after===undefined), or did the command stop mutating the config (before===after)?
The needs:["setting"] gate ensures that on a bridge without the capability this
SKIPS cleanly rather than producing a misleading red.
evidence
{
  "before": true,
  "after": false
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r8-rust-problems.open.png

505 / 564 · r8-rust-problems.open.png

View: Open Problems (Errors and Warnings)

rust · +rust — Rust toolchain + rust-analyzer extension
problems.open
PASS
happened
Problems view opened (diagnostics: 0)
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/viewsSettings.mjs
timing
{"effect":10021}
machine
{"procs":"12→12","memMiB":0,"fsChanges":74,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":5000,"tx":0}}
look for
WHAT: Invokes 'workbench.actions.view.problems' via env.act to open the Problems
(Errors and Warnings) view, then observes and surfaces the current diagnostics
count. The assertion is that the command resolved; the diagnostics count is
reported as evidence (with an "n/a" fallback when the Snapshot omits it).

WHY THIS OUTCOME: Opening the Problems view is a focus/reveal operation — it shows
the diagnostics panel but does not itself create or clear diagnostics. The Snapshot
contract (§3.3) does not expose which view currently has focus, so there is no
faithful way to assert "the Problems view is now focused"; the only guaranteed
truth is that VS Code accepted the built-in command id and ran it, which env.act
enforces by throwing on !ok. Reporting diagnostics (rather than asserting on it) is
correct because the count depends entirely on the scenario's workspace state and is
not something this command changes — so it is evidence, not an assertion target.

WHY IT MATTERS: This is a smoke test for the Problems-view command id and the
diagnostics field of the Snapshot. If VS Code renames/retires the command, or a
bridge refactor breaks command dispatch, env.act throws and this goes red — telling
a future reader the failure is in command wiring, not in diagnostics collection.
The surfaced diagnostics count also doubles as a cheap probe that the bridge's
diagnostics plumbing is alive; an unexpected "n/a" where a number was expected hints
the Snapshot's diagnostics field regressed, independent of the Problems command
itself.
evidence
{
  "diagnostics": 0
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r7-node-visual.workbenchTabSmoke.full.png

509 / 564 · r7-node-visual.workbenchTabSmoke.full.png

Visual: workbench renders with an editor tab and nonblank screenshot

node · +node — Node toolchain + JS/TS extension
visual.workbenchTabSmoke
PASS
happened
workbench visible with 1 tab(s); screenshot 1280x720, blackish=0.0003, buckets=67
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/visualSmoke.mjs
timing
{"effect":8188}
machine
{"procs":"13→13","memMiB":1,"fsChanges":79,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":9000,"tx":0}}
look for
WHAT: Boots one Fleet environment, writes and opens a real file so VS Code must
show an editor tab, then uses Playwright DOM checks plus a decoded screenshot to
assert that the workbench chrome is present and the captured page is not blank or
all-black.

WHY THIS IS THE EXPECTED OUTCOME: A healthy VS Code / code-server workbench with
one opened file has a .monaco-workbench root, an editor part, a tab row, an
activity bar, and a status bar. Its screenshot contains many colour buckets and
not an overwhelming fraction of near-black pixels. Those are stable visual facts
that do not depend on exact theme colours or text rendering.

WHY IT MATTERS: Bridge command/query tests can pass while the user-facing page is
visually broken. This catches the class of regressions where the editor starts
and phones home but the browser surface is blank, black, missing tabs, or missing
core workbench chrome. It is intentionally a coarse smoke test: exact pixel
matching would be brittle across VS Code/theme/browser versions.
evidence
{
  "screenshot": "artifacts/r7-node-visual.workbenchTabSmoke.full.png",
  "tabCount": 1,
  "activityBarCount": 1,
  "editorCount": 1,
  "statusBarCount": 1,
  "stats": {
    "decoded": true,
    "width": 1280,
    "height": 720,
    "blackishRatio": 0.0003,
    "lightRatio": 0.9884,
    "colorBuckets": 67
  }
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r7-node-visual.workbenchTabSmoke.result.png

510 / 564 · r7-node-visual.workbenchTabSmoke.result.png

Visual: workbench renders with an editor tab and nonblank screenshot

node · +node — Node toolchain + JS/TS extension
visual.workbenchTabSmoke
PASS
happened
workbench visible with 1 tab(s); screenshot 1280x720, blackish=0.0003, buckets=67
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/visualSmoke.mjs
timing
{"effect":8188}
machine
{"procs":"13→13","memMiB":1,"fsChanges":79,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":9000,"tx":0}}
look for
WHAT: Boots one Fleet environment, writes and opens a real file so VS Code must
show an editor tab, then uses Playwright DOM checks plus a decoded screenshot to
assert that the workbench chrome is present and the captured page is not blank or
all-black.

WHY THIS IS THE EXPECTED OUTCOME: A healthy VS Code / code-server workbench with
one opened file has a .monaco-workbench root, an editor part, a tab row, an
activity bar, and a status bar. Its screenshot contains many colour buckets and
not an overwhelming fraction of near-black pixels. Those are stable visual facts
that do not depend on exact theme colours or text rendering.

WHY IT MATTERS: Bridge command/query tests can pass while the user-facing page is
visually broken. This catches the class of regressions where the editor starts
and phones home but the browser surface is blank, black, missing tabs, or missing
core workbench chrome. It is intentionally a coarse smoke test: exact pixel
matching would be brittle across VS Code/theme/browser versions.
evidence
{
  "screenshot": "artifacts/r7-node-visual.workbenchTabSmoke.full.png",
  "tabCount": 1,
  "activityBarCount": 1,
  "editorCount": 1,
  "statusBarCount": 1,
  "stats": {
    "decoded": true,
    "width": 1280,
    "height": 720,
    "blackishRatio": 0.0003,
    "lightRatio": 0.9884,
    "colorBuckets": 67
  }
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r8-rust-settings.toggleMinimap.png

511 / 564 · r8-rust-settings.toggleMinimap.png

Settings: Toggle Minimap flips editor.minimap.enabled

rust · +rust — Rust toolchain + rust-analyzer extension
settings.toggleMinimap
PASS
happened
editor.minimap.enabled true → false
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/viewsSettings.mjs
timing
{"effect":14035}
machine
{"procs":"12→12","memMiB":14,"fsChanges":79,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":63000,"tx":110000}}
look for
WHAT: Reads editor.minimap.enabled via the 'setting' bridge query, runs
'editor.action.toggleMinimap' via env.act, re-reads the setting, and asserts the
value actually flipped (before !== after && after !== undefined). Unlike the other
behaviours in this file, this one makes a REAL round-trip assertion on observable
state, so it declares needs:["setting"] and is SKIPPED (not failed) when the bridge
does not advertise the 'setting' capability.

WHY THIS OUTCOME: Minimap was chosen deliberately over word-wrap. toggleWordWrap
sets a transient PER-EDITOR override that config.get / the 'setting' query never
reflect, so it is fundamentally unverifiable through this path — a test on it would
either always pass vacuously or always fail. toggleMinimap, by contrast, mutates
the actual editor.minimap.enabled configuration value, which the 'setting' query
reads back. Because it is a boolean toggle, a correct implementation MUST produce
before !== after; if the value is undefined afterward, the setting wasn't readable
at all. The settingValue() helper tolerates both result shapes (top-level r.value
and nested r.data.value per §3.3) so the assertion doesn't hinge on which Track-E
encoding the bridge happens to use.

WHY IT MATTERS: This is the one end-to-end proof in the file that a settings-mutating
command's effect is observable through the bridge's read path — command write +
setting read, the full loop. It guards three things at once: (1) the toggleMinimap
command still maps to editor.minimap.enabled, (2) the 'setting' query returns the
live value in a shape settingValue() understands, and (3) the choice of a
config-backed (not per-editor-override) setting stays correct. A future reader
seeing this fail should check, in order: did the setting-query result shape change
(after===undefined), or did the command stop mutating the config (before===after)?
The needs:["setting"] gate ensures that on a bridge without the capability this
SKIPS cleanly rather than producing a misleading red.
evidence
{
  "before": true,
  "after": false
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r10-mem-capped-palette.open.png

515 / 564 · r10-mem-capped-palette.open.png

Command Palette opens

mem-capped · Memory capped — 512m (boots degraded under pressure)
palette.open
PASS
happened
executeCommand(showCommands) returned ok
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/core.mjs
timing
{"effect":13916}
machine
{"procs":"10→10","memMiB":0,"fsChanges":66,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":8000,"tx":10000}}
look for
WHAT: Drives the bridge to executeCommand("workbench.action.showCommands") —
the built-in VS Code action behind the Command Palette (Ctrl/Cmd+Shift+P) — and
asserts only that the call returns ok (env.act throws on a non-ok bridge reply,
so reaching the return is itself the assertion). It then observe()s under the
"palette.open" tag to capture a screenshot/snapshot for the report.

WHY THIS IS THE EXPECTED OUTCOME: "workbench.action.showCommands" is one of the
most fundamental, always-registered commands in a VS Code / code-server
workbench; it has no preconditions (no file open, no selection, no extension
required) and never fails on a healthy editor host. So a successful, error-free
executeCommand round-trip is exactly what a live, responsive workbench produces.
We deliberately do NOT assert palette-visible state here: the quick-input widget
is a transient overlay that the headless snapshot does not reliably expose, so
asserting on it would be flaky. The honest, stable contract at this layer is
"the editor accepted and ran a core command".

WHY IT MATTERS: This is the smoke test that proves the whole bridge round-trip is
alive — JSON-RPC/WebSocket from harness → reporter → VS Code extension host →
command execution → ok reply. If the container, the bridge wiring, or command
registration regresses (e.g. a refactor breaks the act() transport, the
extension fails to activate, or the workbench never finishes booting), this is
the first and cheapest test to go red. A future reader seeing this fail should
suspect the transport/activation layer itself, not palette-specific logic,
before touching any higher-level behaviour.

WHY IT MATTERS: This is the smoke test that proves the whole bridge round-trip is

missing screenshot: artifacts/r10-mem-capped-file.openWelcomeClose.before.png

521 / 564 · r10-mem-capped-file.openWelcomeClose.before.png

Close the active (Welcome) tab → open tab count shrinks

mem-capped · Memory capped — 512m (boots degraded under pressure)
file.openWelcomeClose
PASS
happened
openTabs 1 → 0
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/files.mjs
timing
{"effect":10213}
machine
{"procs":"10→10","memMiB":0,"fsChanges":66,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":6000,"tx":10000}}
look for
WHAT: On a fresh workbench (where the VS Code Welcome/Get-Started tab is open by
default), captures `openTabs.length`, fires the real `workbench.action.closeActiveEditor`
command, then asserts the count strictly decreased (afterN < beforeN). If the snapshot
doesn't expose `openTabs` at all it reports "not measurable" rather than asserting.

WHY THIS IS CORRECT: `closeActiveEditor` closes exactly the focused editor/tab; on a
fresh window the focused tab IS the Welcome tab, so the open-tab set must shrink by one.
We assert a *delta* (after < before) rather than a hard "tabs == N" because the exact
fresh-window tab count is environment-dependent (extensions, restored editors) — the
only invariant we can rely on is "closing removed one". The measurable-guard exists
because this behaviour runs with baseline {command,query} caps only and must degrade
honestly if the snapshot shape changes.

WHY IT MATTERS: This is the smoke test (tagged "smoke") that proves the command channel
round-trips a genuine workbench mutation AND that the snapshot reflects editor lifecycle
changes. If a refactor breaks the bridge's command dispatch, or makes `openTabs` stale
(cached and never invalidated on close), this is the cheapest, earliest tripwire — a
break here implies the entire observe→act→observe loop is untrustworthy for every other
files test, so it's deliberately kept dependency-free.
evidence
{
  "beforeTabs": [
    "Welcome"
  ],
  "afterTabs": []
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r10-mem-capped-file.openWelcomeClose.after.png

[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/files.mjs

machine
{"procs":"10→10","memMiB":0,"fsChanges":66,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":6000,"tx":10000}}
look for
WHAT: On a fresh workbench (where the VS Code Welcome/Get-Started tab is open by
default), captures `openTabs.length`, fires the real `workbench.action.closeActiveEditor`
command, then asserts the count strictly decreased (afterN < beforeN). If the snapshot
doesn't expose `openTabs` at all it reports "not measurable" rather than asserting.

WHY THIS IS CORRECT: `closeActiveEditor` closes exactly the focused editor/tab; on a
fresh window the focused tab IS the Welcome tab, so the open-tab set must shrink by one.
We assert a *delta* (after < before) rather than a hard "tabs == N" because the exact
fresh-window tab count is environment-dependent (extensions, restored editors) — the
only invariant we can rely on is "closing removed one". The measurable-guard exists
because this behaviour runs with baseline {command,query} caps only and must degrade
honestly if the snapshot shape changes.

WHY IT MATTERS: This is the smoke test (tagged "smoke") that proves the command channel
round-trips a genuine workbench mutation AND that the snapshot reflects editor lifecycle
changes. If a refactor breaks the bridge's command dispatch, or makes `openTabs` stale
(cached and never invalidated on close), this is the cheapest, earliest tripwire — a
break here implies the entire observe→act→observe loop is untrustworthy for every other
files test, so it's deliberately kept dependency-free.
evidence
{
  "beforeTabs": [
    "Welcome"
  ],
  "afterTabs": []
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r10-mem-capped-file.openWelcomeClose.result.png

523 / 564 · r10-mem-capped-file.openWelcomeClose.result.png

Close the active (Welcome) tab → open tab count shrinks

mem-capped · Memory capped — 512m (boots degraded under pressure)
file.openWelcomeClose
PASS
happened
openTabs 1 → 0
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/files.mjs
timing
{"effect":10213}
machine
{"procs":"10→10","memMiB":0,"fsChanges":66,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":6000,"tx":10000}}
look for
WHAT: On a fresh workbench (where the VS Code Welcome/Get-Started tab is open by
default), captures `openTabs.length`, fires the real `workbench.action.closeActiveEditor`
command, then asserts the count strictly decreased (afterN < beforeN). If the snapshot
doesn't expose `openTabs` at all it reports "not measurable" rather than asserting.

WHY THIS IS CORRECT: `closeActiveEditor` closes exactly the focused editor/tab; on a
fresh window the focused tab IS the Welcome tab, so the open-tab set must shrink by one.
We assert a *delta* (after < before) rather than a hard "tabs == N" because the exact
fresh-window tab count is environment-dependent (extensions, restored editors) — the
only invariant we can rely on is "closing removed one". The measurable-guard exists
because this behaviour runs with baseline {command,query} caps only and must degrade
honestly if the snapshot shape changes.

WHY IT MATTERS: This is the smoke test (tagged "smoke") that proves the command channel
round-trips a genuine workbench mutation AND that the snapshot reflects editor lifecycle
changes. If a refactor breaks the bridge's command dispatch, or makes `openTabs` stale
(cached and never invalidated on close), this is the cheapest, earliest tripwire — a
break here implies the entire observe→act→observe loop is untrustworthy for every other
files test, so it's deliberately kept dependency-free.
evidence
{
  "beforeTabs": [
    "Welcome"
  ],
  "afterTabs": []
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r10-mem-capped-search.findInFiles.before.png

524 / 564 · r10-mem-capped-search.findInFiles.before.png

Search: open the Find-in-Files view

mem-capped · Memory capped — 512m (boots degraded under pressure)
search.findInFiles
PASS
happened
executeCommand(findInFiles) returned ok (no view in snapshot to assert)
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/searchGit.mjs
timing
{"effect":8198}
machine
{"procs":"10→10","memMiB":0,"fsChanges":66,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":4000,"tx":0}}
look for
WHAT: Observes the active/focused view before, executes the built-in
"workbench.action.findInFiles" command via the bridge, waits 1.2s for the view
to settle, then re-observes. If the snapshot exposes activeView/focusedView, it
asserts that view matches /search/i; if the snapshot can't tell us the view, it
degrades to "the command ran ok" (env.act throws on a non-ok reply, so reaching
the return is the assertion). Evidence records before/after view names.

WHY THIS IS THE EXPECTED OUTCOME: "workbench.action.findInFiles" is the canonical
command that reveals the Search viewlet in the sidebar and focuses its query box.
On a healthy workbench it always succeeds and, when the view layer is queryable,
the active view becomes the Search view — hence the /search/i match. The dual
posture (assert-if-known, else ok) is intentional: Track-E may or may not surface
the active view in the snapshot, and we refuse to hard-fail a real success just
because the harness can't see the viewlet name. That mirrors the proven
palette.open baseline.

WHY IT MATTERS: This guards the Search feature's entry point. If a refactor
unregisters/renames the command, breaks viewlet activation, or the snapshot's
view-reporting drifts, this test catches it. A future reader seeing the /search/i
branch fail should check whether the workbench still maps findInFiles to the
Search viewlet (or whether the snapshot's view field changed shape); seeing the
fallback branch always taken means the snapshot lost its view introspection and
the assertion has silently weakened — worth restoring.
evidence
{
  "beforeView": null,
  "afterView": null
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r11-cpu-capped-palette.open.png

527 / 564 · r11-cpu-capped-palette.open.png

Command Palette opens

cpu-capped · CPU capped — 0.5 cores (boots, slower)
palette.open
PASS
happened
executeCommand(showCommands) returned ok
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/core.mjs
timing
{"effect":11769}
machine
{"procs":"10→10","memMiB":3,"fsChanges":66,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":6000,"tx":0}}
look for
WHAT: Drives the bridge to executeCommand("workbench.action.showCommands") —
the built-in VS Code action behind the Command Palette (Ctrl/Cmd+Shift+P) — and
asserts only that the call returns ok (env.act throws on a non-ok bridge reply,
so reaching the return is itself the assertion). It then observe()s under the
"palette.open" tag to capture a screenshot/snapshot for the report.

WHY THIS IS THE EXPECTED OUTCOME: "workbench.action.showCommands" is one of the
most fundamental, always-registered commands in a VS Code / code-server
workbench; it has no preconditions (no file open, no selection, no extension
required) and never fails on a healthy editor host. So a successful, error-free
executeCommand round-trip is exactly what a live, responsive workbench produces.
We deliberately do NOT assert palette-visible state here: the quick-input widget
is a transient overlay that the headless snapshot does not reliably expose, so
asserting on it would be flaky. The honest, stable contract at this layer is
"the editor accepted and ran a core command".

WHY IT MATTERS: This is the smoke test that proves the whole bridge round-trip is
alive — JSON-RPC/WebSocket from harness → reporter → VS Code extension host →
command execution → ok reply. If the container, the bridge wiring, or command
registration regresses (e.g. a refactor breaks the act() transport, the
extension fails to activate, or the workbench never finishes booting), this is
the first and cheapest test to go red. A future reader seeing this fail should
suspect the transport/activation layer itself, not palette-specific logic,
before touching any higher-level behaviour.

WHY IT MATTERS: This is the smoke test that proves the whole bridge round-trip is

missing screenshot: artifacts/r10-mem-capped-terminal.new.before.png

529 / 564 · r10-mem-capped-terminal.new.before.png

Terminal: New Terminal opens a terminal

mem-capped · Memory capped — 512m (boots degraded under pressure)
terminal.new
PASS
happened
terminals 0 → 1 (["bash"])
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/terminal.mjs
timing
{"effect":11950}
machine
{"procs":"10→12","memMiB":34,"fsChanges":68,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":121000,"tx":190000}}
look for
WHAT: Snapshots terminalCount, fires the built-in command
'workbench.action.terminal.new' through the bridge act() channel, waits 2s, and
asserts the post-action terminalCount is strictly greater than the pre-action
count. This is the single most basic terminal assertion in the suite — "an
integrated terminal actually came into existence when we asked for one."

WHY THIS IS THE EXPECTED OUTCOME: 'workbench.action.terminal.new' is VS Code's
canonical command for creating an integrated terminal. When invoked it spawns a
backing pty/shell process and registers a Terminal object with the workbench;
our query bridge reads window.terminals and reports terminalCount. So a healthy
env must show count rising by (at least) one. We assert ">" rather than "== +1"
deliberately: this is the SHARED-isolation smoke test, so the env may already
carry terminals from earlier behaviours — the only invariant we can rely on is
monotonic growth, not an exact value. The 2s sleep covers the async gap between
the command resolving and the pty registering in the snapshot.

WHY IT MATTERS: This is the proven baseline ported straight from harness.mjs
(terminalCount 0→1) and the canary for the entire bridge round-trip — if act()
can dispatch a command, VS Code can execute it, and query() can observe the
result, then the whole command→effect→observation pipeline is alive. If this
ever breaks after a refactor, suspect the bridge wiring (act/query channel,
command id rename, or the query no longer reading window.terminals) BEFORE
suspecting terminals themselves; every richer terminal behaviour builds on this
exact mechanism, so a green here narrows a failure elsewhere to that behaviour's
own specifics.
evidence
{
  "before": {
    "terminalCount": 0,
    "terminals": []
  },
  "after": {
    "terminalCount": 1,
    "terminals": [
      "bash"
    ]
  }
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r10-mem-capped-view.toggleSidebar.before.png

535 / 564 · r10-mem-capped-view.toggleSidebar.before.png

View: Toggle Primary Side Bar

mem-capped · Memory capped — 512m (boots degraded under pressure)
view.toggleSidebar
PASS
happened
toggleSidebarVisibility resolved (visibility not yet in Snapshot)
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/viewsSettings.mjs
timing
{"effect":10017}
machine
{"procs":"12→12","memMiB":0,"fsChanges":68,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":5000,"tx":10000}}
look for
WHAT: Drives the real VS Code command 'workbench.action.toggleSidebarVisibility'
through the bridge (env.act, which throws on a non-ok executeCommand result) and
asserts only that the command resolved. We snapshot visibleEditors before and
after as evidence; we do NOT assert sidebar visibility itself because the Snapshot
contract (§3.3) does not yet expose it (a Track-D upgrade may add it).

WHY THIS OUTCOME: The primary side bar is a workbench-chrome element, not an
editor or a terminal. Toggling its visibility is a pure layout operation — it does
not open/close editors, so visibleEditors is expected to be stable across the
toggle. The only thing we can faithfully verify today is that VS Code accepted and
executed the built-in command id without error, which env.act guarantees by
rejecting on !ok. Asserting pass:true unconditionally is correct here precisely
because the success signal IS "the command resolved"; inventing a visibility
assertion against a field the bridge can't supply would be a false test.

WHY IT MATTERS: This guards the command-dispatch path itself — that the bridge can
still reach the VS Code command registry and run a stock workbench command end to
end. If a refactor of the bridge transport, the executeCommand wrapper, or the
command-id wiring breaks, env.act will throw and this smoke test goes red even
though it makes no rich assertion. A future reader seeing this fail should suspect
the bridge/command plumbing, not the sidebar feature. The recorded before/after
visibleEditors also documents the baseline assumption (sidebar toggling is
editor-neutral) so that if Track-D later adds a real visibility check, the expected
invariants are already written down.
evidence
{
  "beforeVisibleEditors": [],
  "afterVisibleEditors": []
}

536 / 564 · r10-mem-capped-view.toggleSidebar.after.png

View: Toggle Primary Side Bar

mem-capped · Memory capped — 512m (boots degraded under pressure)
view.toggleSidebar
PASS
happened
toggleSidebarVisibility resolved (visibility not yet in Snapshot)
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/viewsSettings.mjs
timing
{"effect":10017}
machine
{"procs":"12→12","memMiB":0,"fsChanges":68,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":5000,"tx":10000}}
look for
WHAT: Drives the real VS Code command 'workbench.action.toggleSidebarVisibility'
through the bridge (env.act, which throws on a non-ok executeCommand result) and
asserts only that the command resolved. We snapshot visibleEditors before and
after as evidence; we do NOT assert sidebar visibility itself because the Snapshot
contract (§3.3) does not yet expose it (a Track-D upgrade may add it).

WHY THIS OUTCOME: The primary side bar is a workbench-chrome element, not an
editor or a terminal. Toggling its visibility is a pure layout operation — it does
not open/close editors, so visibleEditors is expected to be stable across the
toggle. The only thing we can faithfully verify today is that VS Code accepted and
executed the built-in command id without error, which env.act guarantees by
rejecting on !ok. Asserting pass:true unconditionally is correct here precisely
because the success signal IS "the command resolved"; inventing a visibility
assertion against a field the bridge can't supply would be a false test.

WHY IT MATTERS: This guards the command-dispatch path itself — that the bridge can
still reach the VS Code command registry and run a stock workbench command end to
end. If a refactor of the bridge transport, the executeCommand wrapper, or the
command-id wiring breaks, env.act will throw and this smoke test goes red even
though it makes no rich assertion. A future reader seeing this fail should suspect
the bridge/command plumbing, not the sidebar feature. The recorded before/after
visibleEditors also documents the baseline assumption (sidebar toggling is
editor-neutral) so that if Track-D later adds a real visibility check, the expected
invariants are already written down.
evidence
{
  "beforeVisibleEditors": [],
  "afterVisibleEditors": []
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r10-mem-capped-view.togglePanel.before.png

538 / 564 · r10-mem-capped-view.togglePanel.before.png

View: Toggle Panel

mem-capped · Memory capped — 512m (boots degraded under pressure)
view.togglePanel
PASS
happened
togglePanel resolved (terminalCount 1 → 1, unchanged by hide)
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/viewsSettings.mjs
timing
{"effect":8283}
machine
{"procs":"12→12","memMiB":0,"fsChanges":68,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":3000,"tx":0}}
look for
WHAT: Runs 'workbench.action.togglePanel' via env.act and asserts the command
resolved, capturing terminalCount before and after as evidence. We explicitly do
NOT assert that the panel's visibility changed (no Snapshot field for it yet) and
we explicitly assert that terminalCount is the relevant invariant to surface.

WHY THIS OUTCOME: The bottom panel is the host surface for the integrated terminal
(among other views). A common but wrong mental model is "hiding the panel kills the
terminal" — in VS Code the terminal process keeps running when its hosting panel is
toggled closed; only the UI is hidden. So terminalCount is expected to be UNCHANGED
across the toggle. That is why the detail line reports the count transition as
"unchanged by hide": the correct behaviour is that toggling the panel is a
visibility-only operation with no lifecycle effect on terminals. Asserting pass on
command resolution is the honest assertion given the Snapshot can't report panel
visibility today.

WHY IT MATTERS: This pins down the contract that panel visibility and terminal
lifecycle are decoupled. If a refactor (e.g. of terminal disposal, of the
panel-show/hide handlers, or of how the env counts terminals) ever caused hiding
the panel to dispose terminals, terminalCount would drift and the evidence here
would expose it even before a dedicated visibility check exists. A future reader
debugging a terminal-count regression can use the before/after evidence to decide
whether the panel toggle is implicated. It also guards the same command-dispatch
plumbing as toggleSidebar — a thrown env.act means the bridge lost the ability to
run stock workbench commands.
evidence
{
  "beforeTerminalCount": 1,
  "afterTerminalCount": 1
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r11-cpu-capped-search.findInFiles.before.png

541 / 564 · r11-cpu-capped-search.findInFiles.before.png

Search: open the Find-in-Files view

cpu-capped · CPU capped — 0.5 cores (boots, slower)
search.findInFiles
PASS
happened
executeCommand(findInFiles) returned ok (no view in snapshot to assert)
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/searchGit.mjs
timing
{"effect":14162}
machine
{"procs":"10→10","memMiB":-1,"fsChanges":66,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":6000,"tx":0}}
look for
WHAT: Observes the active/focused view before, executes the built-in
"workbench.action.findInFiles" command via the bridge, waits 1.2s for the view
to settle, then re-observes. If the snapshot exposes activeView/focusedView, it
asserts that view matches /search/i; if the snapshot can't tell us the view, it
degrades to "the command ran ok" (env.act throws on a non-ok reply, so reaching
the return is the assertion). Evidence records before/after view names.

WHY THIS IS THE EXPECTED OUTCOME: "workbench.action.findInFiles" is the canonical
command that reveals the Search viewlet in the sidebar and focuses its query box.
On a healthy workbench it always succeeds and, when the view layer is queryable,
the active view becomes the Search view — hence the /search/i match. The dual
posture (assert-if-known, else ok) is intentional: Track-E may or may not surface
the active view in the snapshot, and we refuse to hard-fail a real success just
because the harness can't see the viewlet name. That mirrors the proven
palette.open baseline.

WHY IT MATTERS: This guards the Search feature's entry point. If a refactor
unregisters/renames the command, breaks viewlet activation, or the snapshot's
view-reporting drifts, this test catches it. A future reader seeing the /search/i
branch fail should check whether the workbench still maps findInFiles to the
Search viewlet (or whether the snapshot's view field changed shape); seeing the
fallback branch always taken means the snapshot lost its view introspection and
the assertion has silently weakened — worth restoring.
evidence
{
  "beforeView": null,
  "afterView": null
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r10-mem-capped-problems.open.png

544 / 564 · r10-mem-capped-problems.open.png

View: Open Problems (Errors and Warnings)

mem-capped · Memory capped — 512m (boots degraded under pressure)
problems.open
PASS
happened
Problems view opened (diagnostics: 0)
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/viewsSettings.mjs
timing
{"effect":8018}
machine
{"procs":"12→12","memMiB":0,"fsChanges":68,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":4000,"tx":10000}}
look for
WHAT: Invokes 'workbench.actions.view.problems' via env.act to open the Problems
(Errors and Warnings) view, then observes and surfaces the current diagnostics
count. The assertion is that the command resolved; the diagnostics count is
reported as evidence (with an "n/a" fallback when the Snapshot omits it).

WHY THIS OUTCOME: Opening the Problems view is a focus/reveal operation — it shows
the diagnostics panel but does not itself create or clear diagnostics. The Snapshot
contract (§3.3) does not expose which view currently has focus, so there is no
faithful way to assert "the Problems view is now focused"; the only guaranteed
truth is that VS Code accepted the built-in command id and ran it, which env.act
enforces by throwing on !ok. Reporting diagnostics (rather than asserting on it) is
correct because the count depends entirely on the scenario's workspace state and is
not something this command changes — so it is evidence, not an assertion target.

WHY IT MATTERS: This is a smoke test for the Problems-view command id and the
diagnostics field of the Snapshot. If VS Code renames/retires the command, or a
bridge refactor breaks command dispatch, env.act throws and this goes red — telling
a future reader the failure is in command wiring, not in diagnostics collection.
The surfaced diagnostics count also doubles as a cheap probe that the bridge's
diagnostics plumbing is alive; an unexpected "n/a" where a number was expected hints
the Snapshot's diagnostics field regressed, independent of the Problems command
itself.
evidence
{
  "diagnostics": 0
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r10-mem-capped-settings.toggleMinimap.png

546 / 564 · r10-mem-capped-settings.toggleMinimap.png

Settings: Toggle Minimap flips editor.minimap.enabled

mem-capped · Memory capped — 512m (boots degraded under pressure)
settings.toggleMinimap
PASS
happened
editor.minimap.enabled true → false
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/viewsSettings.mjs
timing
{"effect":6248}
machine
{"procs":"12→12","memMiB":17,"fsChanges":73,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":55000,"tx":100000}}
look for
WHAT: Reads editor.minimap.enabled via the 'setting' bridge query, runs
'editor.action.toggleMinimap' via env.act, re-reads the setting, and asserts the
value actually flipped (before !== after && after !== undefined). Unlike the other
behaviours in this file, this one makes a REAL round-trip assertion on observable
state, so it declares needs:["setting"] and is SKIPPED (not failed) when the bridge
does not advertise the 'setting' capability.

WHY THIS OUTCOME: Minimap was chosen deliberately over word-wrap. toggleWordWrap
sets a transient PER-EDITOR override that config.get / the 'setting' query never
reflect, so it is fundamentally unverifiable through this path — a test on it would
either always pass vacuously or always fail. toggleMinimap, by contrast, mutates
the actual editor.minimap.enabled configuration value, which the 'setting' query
reads back. Because it is a boolean toggle, a correct implementation MUST produce
before !== after; if the value is undefined afterward, the setting wasn't readable
at all. The settingValue() helper tolerates both result shapes (top-level r.value
and nested r.data.value per §3.3) so the assertion doesn't hinge on which Track-E
encoding the bridge happens to use.

WHY IT MATTERS: This is the one end-to-end proof in the file that a settings-mutating
command's effect is observable through the bridge's read path — command write +
setting read, the full loop. It guards three things at once: (1) the toggleMinimap
command still maps to editor.minimap.enabled, (2) the 'setting' query returns the
live value in a shape settingValue() understands, and (3) the choice of a
config-backed (not per-editor-override) setting stays correct. A future reader
seeing this fail should check, in order: did the setting-query result shape change
(after===undefined), or did the command stop mutating the config (before===after)?
The needs:["setting"] gate ensures that on a bridge without the capability this
SKIPS cleanly rather than producing a misleading red.
evidence
{
  "before": true,
  "after": false
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r11-cpu-capped-terminal.new.before.png

548 / 564 · r11-cpu-capped-terminal.new.before.png

Terminal: New Terminal opens a terminal

cpu-capped · CPU capped — 0.5 cores (boots, slower)
terminal.new
PASS
happened
terminals 0 → 1 (["bash"])
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/terminal.mjs
timing
{"effect":16238}
machine
{"procs":"10→12","memMiB":36,"fsChanges":68,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":123000,"tx":190000}}
look for
WHAT: Snapshots terminalCount, fires the built-in command
'workbench.action.terminal.new' through the bridge act() channel, waits 2s, and
asserts the post-action terminalCount is strictly greater than the pre-action
count. This is the single most basic terminal assertion in the suite — "an
integrated terminal actually came into existence when we asked for one."

WHY THIS IS THE EXPECTED OUTCOME: 'workbench.action.terminal.new' is VS Code's
canonical command for creating an integrated terminal. When invoked it spawns a
backing pty/shell process and registers a Terminal object with the workbench;
our query bridge reads window.terminals and reports terminalCount. So a healthy
env must show count rising by (at least) one. We assert ">" rather than "== +1"
deliberately: this is the SHARED-isolation smoke test, so the env may already
carry terminals from earlier behaviours — the only invariant we can rely on is
monotonic growth, not an exact value. The 2s sleep covers the async gap between
the command resolving and the pty registering in the snapshot.

WHY IT MATTERS: This is the proven baseline ported straight from harness.mjs
(terminalCount 0→1) and the canary for the entire bridge round-trip — if act()
can dispatch a command, VS Code can execute it, and query() can observe the
result, then the whole command→effect→observation pipeline is alive. If this
ever breaks after a refactor, suspect the bridge wiring (act/query channel,
command id rename, or the query no longer reading window.terminals) BEFORE
suspecting terminals themselves; every richer terminal behaviour builds on this
exact mechanism, so a green here narrows a failure elsewhere to that behaviour's
own specifics.
evidence
{
  "before": {
    "terminalCount": 0,
    "terminals": []
  },
  "after": {
    "terminalCount": 1,
    "terminals": [
      "bash"
    ]
  }
}

deliberately: this is the SHARED-isolation smoke test, so the env may already
carry terminals from earlier behaviours — the only invariant we can rely on is
monotonic growth, not an exact value. The 2s sleep covers the async gap between
the command resolving and the pty registering in the snapshot.

WHY IT MATTERS: This is the proven baseline ported straight from harness.mjs
(terminalCount 0→1) and the canary for the entire bridge round-trip — if act()
can dispatch a command, VS Code can execute it, and query() can observe the
result, then the whole command→effect→observation pipeline is alive. If this
ever breaks after a refactor, suspect the bridge wiring (act/query channel,
command id rename, or the query no longer reading window.terminals) BEFORE
suspecting terminals themselves; every richer terminal behaviour builds on this
exact mechanism, so a green here narrows a failure elsewhere to that behaviour's
own specifics.

{

    "terminalCount": 0,
    "terminals": []
  },
  "after": {

missing screenshot: artifacts/r10-mem-capped-visual.workbenchTabSmoke.full.png

551 / 564 · r10-mem-capped-visual.workbenchTabSmoke.full.png

Visual: workbench renders with an editor tab and nonblank screenshot

mem-capped · Memory capped — 512m (boots degraded under pressure)
visual.workbenchTabSmoke
PASS
happened
workbench visible with 1 tab(s); screenshot 1280x720, blackish=0.0003, buckets=67
changed
[uncommitted·—] containers/fleet-env/eval/behaviours/visualSmoke.mjs
timing
{"effect":6091}
machine
{"procs":"12→12","memMiB":0,"fsChanges":74,"diskMiB":0,"netBytes":{"rx":6000,"tx":10000}}
look for
WHAT: Boots one Fleet environment, writes and opens a real file so VS Code must
show an editor tab, then uses Playwright DOM checks plus a decoded screenshot to
assert that the workbench chrome is present and the captured page is not blank or
all-black.

WHY THIS IS THE EXPECTED OUTCOME: A healthy VS Code / code-server workbench with
one opened file has a .monaco-workbench root, an editor part, a tab row, an
activity bar, and a status bar. Its screenshot contains many colour buckets and
not an overwhelming fraction of near-black pixels. Those are stable visual facts
that do not depend on exact theme colours or text rendering.

WHY IT MATTERS: Bridge command/query tests can pass while the user-facing page is
visually broken. This catches the class of regressions where the editor starts
and phones home but the browser surface is blank, black, missing tabs, or missing
core workbench chrome. It is intentionally a coarse smoke test: exact pixel
matching would be brittle across VS Code/theme/browser versions.
evidence
{
  "screenshot": "artifacts/r10-mem-capped-visual.workbenchTabSmoke.full.png",
  "tabCount": 1,
  "activityBarCount": 1,
  "editorCount": 1,
  "statusBarCount": 1,
  "stats": {
    "decoded": true,
    "width": 1280,
    "height": 720,
    "blackishRatio": 0.0003,
    "lightRatio": 0.9885,
    "colorBuckets": 67
  }
}

missing screenshot: artifacts/r10-mem-capped-visual.workbenchTabSmoke.result.png

and phones home but the browser surface is blank, black, missing tabs, or missing

{
  "screenshot": "artifacts/r10-mem-capped-visual.workbenchTabSmoke.full.png",
  "tabCount": 1,
  "activityBarCount": 1,
  "editorCount": 1,
  "statusBarCount": 1,

    "width": 1280,
